topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile efficiency of a corporate memo. Subject line: Retirement Announcement. My mentor, Dr. Alistair Finch, was stepping down. No farewell, no mention of the years of guidance, just a curt notification that his tenure had concluded. I stared at the screen, a slow, tight knot forming in my chest. It felt like a deliberate erasure, a dismissal of everything we had built together. I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning under my weight, and let out a breath that was far too sharp. I picked up my pen, the cheap plastic feeling alien in my hand, and began to meticulously reorganize the files he’d left scattered across my desk. Each file, each meticulously labeled folder, was a monument to a relationship that had abruptly ended without ceremony.

[story 2]

When Sarah mentioned Dr. Finch was leaving, I offered a tight, practiced smile. "That's... a change," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally level. Inside, a cold wave washed over me. I watched her eyes, searching for some flicker of regret, some acknowledgment of the shared journey. There was nothing. Just a polite, distant nod. I felt a distinct tightening around my jaw, a physical manifestation of the sheer lack of respect the situation inspired. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, and turned it slowly in a circle. The silence in the office suddenly felt heavy, weighted with unspoken finality.

[story 3]

I found the notice tucked into the corner of his otherwise immaculate office—a stark white piece of paper mocking the vibrant tapestry of shared projects that had defined the last decade. There was no note, no personal touch, just the official declaration of departure. A low, almost inaudible scoff escaped my lips. I stood up, the movement stiff, and walked toward the window, surveying the cityscape as if preparing to discard the view entirely. My shoulders remained rigid. I didn't need an explanation; the message was clear enough. It was a quiet confirmation that the pedestal I had built for him had been removed, and I was left standing in the resulting emptiness.
